text.title: SLOs, Performance Layers, and Capacity Planning
text.summary: SLO tier table (startup to enterprise), five performance layers with target budgets, error budget calculation, and capacity planning formulas for pre-launch sizing.

section.title: SLO Tier Table

rule.statement: Define SLOs from user-impact analysis, not from industry tables. "p95 < 200ms" is a textbook number; the real target comes from measuring at what latency users abandon the page or the transaction.
rule.rationale: LLM-suggested SLOs consistently default to textbook values; teams then optimize to the wrong target. Tie SLOs to user-journey data and competitive benchmarks.

list.item: Startup/MVP: p50 < 200ms, p95 < 500ms, p99 < 2s, throughput > 1k RPS, error rate < 1%, availability 99.9% (43.8 min/month downtime).
Growth: p50 < 100ms, p95 < 300ms, p99 < 1s, throughput > 10k RPS, error rate < 0.1%, availability 99.95% (21.9 min/month).
Enterprise: p50 < 50ms, p95 < 150ms, p99 < 500ms, throughput > 100k RPS, error rate < 0.01%, availability 99.99% (4.4 min/month).

section.note: p99 is where users churn, not mean latency. Always report p95 and p99 together; mean latency is a vanity metric for performance work.

rule.statement: Attribute the bottleneck to exactly one layer before proposing a fix. "The system is slow" is not actionable; "DB query p95 is 180ms, consuming 60% of the request budget" is.
rule.rationale: Layer-agnostic optimizations waste effort — agents rewrite cold code paths because they look messy, not because they are hot. APM traces give layer attribution; flame graphs give code attribution within a layer.

list.item: Layer 1 — CLIENT: browser rendering, JS bundle execution, network RTT. Target: First Contentful Paint < 1.5s, Time to Interactive < 3s. Tools: Lighthouse, WebVitals, bundle analyzer.
Layer 2 — EDGE/CDN: static assets, edge caching, compression, edge functions. Target: cache hit ratio > 95%, edge latency < 50ms globally. Tools: Cloudflare Analytics, CloudFront metrics.
Layer 3 — LOAD BALANCER: request distribution, health checks, SSL termination. Target: connection overhead < 5ms. Tools: nginx/HAProxy metrics, connection pool saturation alerts.

Layer 4 — APPLICATION: code efficiency, concurrency model, async processing, algorithm complexity. Target: request processing < 100ms (excluding I/O wait). Tools: py-spy, async-profiler, distributed traces.

Layer 5 — DATA: query optimization, indexing, connection pooling, cache hit ratio. Target: DB query p95 < 50ms, cache hit ratio > 85%. Tools: pg_stat_statements, EXPLAIN ANALYZE, pgBadger, RedisInsight.
99.9% availability = 43.8 minutes downtime per month, 8.7 hours per year.
99.95% availability = 21.9 minutes per month, 4.4 hours per year.
99.99% availability = 4.4 minutes per month, 52.6 minutes per year.
Error budget = (1 - SLO) × period_duration. Burn through budget faster than the period → freeze feature releases, prioritize reliability work.

rule.statement: Alert on error budget burn rate, not raw availability. A 6x burn rate with 72h remaining in the month demands immediate response; a 1x burn rate is within expectations.
rule.rationale: Raw availability percentages update too slowly; burn rate gives early warning before the budget is exhausted.

list.item: Instance count = design_target_RPS / single_instance_capacity_RPS, rounded up + 1 for N+1 redundancy.
Connection pool size = (core_count × 2) + effective_spindle_count. For SSDs: core_count × 2, typically 10–50 connections per app instance.

example.code:
Peak concurrent users: 100,000 Requests per user per minute: 10 Peak RPS = 100,000 * 10 / 60 = 16,667
Design target (3x): 50,000 RPS Single instance capacity: 500 RPS Required instances: 50,000 / 500 = 100 With N+1 redundancy: 101 With 50% headroom: 150 instances
